title: Your First Project Simulation
subtitle: Launch a Company Blog — End-to-End PM Experience
description: Step into the role of a junior PM managing a real project. You'll create a charter, plan the work, manage stakeholders, and close the project. Each decision affects your project health.

phases.title: Define the Project
phases.scenario: Your manager asks you to launch a company blog within 6 weeks. You have a designer, a developer, and a content writer on your team. Budget: $5,000.
phases.task: Create a project charter defining the project objectives, key deliverables, and stakeholders.

choices.text: Write a detailed charter with objectives, scope, stakeholders, budget, timeline, and success criteria.
choices.outcome: Excellent! A thorough charter gives your team clarity and gets formal sign-off from leadership.

choices.text: Send a quick email summarising the project to the team and get started immediately.
choices.outcome: Moving fast but skipping formal initiation. You'll likely face scope disagreements and unclear ownership later.

choices.text: Ask for a kickoff meeting with all stakeholders before writing anything.
choices.outcome: Good approach! Gathering input first improves your charter quality, though it adds a day.

phases.title: Plan the Work
phases.scenario: Your charter is approved. You need to plan 6 weeks of work across design, development, and content.
phases.task: Break down the project into tasks and create a schedule.

choices.text: Build a full WBS, assign owners, set dependencies, and create a Gantt chart with milestones.
choices.outcome: Thorough planning. Your team knows exactly what to do and when. This pays off during execution.

choices.text: Create a simple Kanban board with To Do / In Progress / Done columns.
choices.outcome: Lightweight but effective for a small team. Good choice for Agile-friendly projects.

choices.text: Let the team members plan their own work and report back weekly.
choices.outcome: Risky. Without shared planning, tasks may overlap or fall through the cracks.

phases.title: Manage a Change Request
phases.scenario: Week 3. The CEO sees the blog mockup and wants to add a podcast section. This would take an extra 2 weeks and $1,500.
phases.task: How do you handle this change request?

choices.text: Evaluate the impact, document a change request, get approval from the sponsor before proceeding.
choices.outcome: Perfect! Formal change control protects your baseline and keeps everyone aligned on the trade-offs.

choices.text: Just add it — the CEO asked for it, so you have to do it.
choices.outcome: Scope creep! Adding work without formal approval puts your timeline and budget at risk. The CEO may not realise the impact.

choices.text: Add it to a Phase 2 backlog and explain why it can't be in the current scope.
choices.outcome: Smart scope management! Negotiating scope boundaries and deferring to Phase 2 keeps the project on track.

phases.title: Track Performance
phases.scenario: Week 4. Development is 50% done but has used 70% of the dev budget. Content is on track.
phases.task: Your CPI for development is 0.71. What do you do?

choices.text: Analyse root cause, raise a flag to the sponsor with options (reduce scope, add budget, accept the risk).
choices.outcome: Excellent! Proactive communication and data-driven options are exactly what good PMs do.

choices.text: Wait and see if the developer can speed up in the next 2 weeks.
choices.outcome: Risky. CPI of 0.71 won't fix itself — waiting makes it worse and surprises stakeholders later.

choices.text: Crash the schedule by bringing in a second developer at extra cost.
choices.outcome: An option, but you've taken unilateral action on budget without sponsor approval. Log it as a corrective action.

phases.title: Close the Project
phases.scenario: Week 6. The blog is live! It got 500 visitors on day 1. Now you need to formally close the project.
phases.task: What do the closing activities include?

choices.text: Get formal stakeholder acceptance, document lessons learned, archive project files, release team, celebrate!
choices.outcome: Perfect closure! You've done it all — formal acceptance, knowledge transfer, team recognition.

choices.text: Send a 'project complete' email and move on to the next project.
choices.outcome: Informal closure misses lessons learned, formal acceptance, and leaves loose ends. Always document your close.

choices.text: Write up the final project report and archive it, but skip the celebration — too busy.
choices.outcome: Good documentation, but don't forget to recognise the team! Morale and retention matter.
